info.topictitle: 3.2.8 Shifts of the AS curve over the long-run
info.parenttitle: 3.2 Variations in economic activity - aggregate demand and aggregate supply

markdown:
<callout type="note">Just as the **SRAS curve** can **shift** in the **short-run**, both the **LRAS curve **and the **Keynesian AS curve** can **shift** in the **long-run**.</callout>

**Shifts** in the **long-run** **aggregate supply** occur when there is **growth** in the **production possibilities **of the economy:

1. If there is an** increase** in the **production possibilities**, the economy has grown its **factors of production** and so can produce more **real output** in the long-run (when there is **full employment** of resources).
2. Therefore, in the **long-run**, there is an increase in the **real output** an economy **produces** at all possible **price levels**.
3. This **shifts** the **LRAS curve** and the **Keynesian AS curve**.

### Shifts in the LRAS curve


1. When there is an **increase **in the **production possibilities** of an economy, the **LRAS curve **shifts to the **right **(LRAS1â†’LRAS2) to a new **potential output **(Yp1â†’Yp2). 
2. When there is a **decrease **in the **production possibilities** of an economy, the **LRAS curve **shifts to the **left **(LRAS1â†’LRAS3) to a new **potential output** (Yp1â†’Yp3). 

![Figure 1: shifts in the LRAS curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/c81f886f64bcb86dd1069de606f647d46d9d1ffa-2398x1732.png)

### Shifts in the Keynesian AS curve


1. When there is an **increase **in the **production possibilities** of an economy, the **Keynesian AS curve **shifts to the **right **(AS1â†’AS2) to a new **potential output **(Yp1â†’Yp2). 
2. When there is a **decrease **in the **production possibilities** of an economy, the **Keynesian AS curve **shifts to the **left **(LRAS1â†’LRAS3) to a new **potential output** (AS1â†’AS3). 

![Figure 2: shifts in the Keynesian AS curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/a4f9589795dfb514ae606365ab7a2fd5fffc7671-2398x1732.png)

## Determinants of aggregate supply over the long-run


1. While changes in the **PL** cause **movements** along the **LRAS curve **/ **Keynesian AS curve**, changes in the **production possibilities** of the economy **shift** the **curves**.
2. The factors that change the **production possibilities** of an economy can hence be understood as the **determinants of aggregate supply over the long-run.**

The **determinants of aggregate supply over the long-run** covered in the IB curriculum are:

1. Changes in the **quantity** and/or **quality** of **factors of production**.
2. **Improvements** in **technology**.
3. **Increases** in **efficiency**.
4. **Changes** in **institutions**.

<callout type="exam_technique">In IB Economics long-answer questions, it is very important to explain processes step-by-step, without skipping any steps.

In this section, the effect of changes in the determinants of SRAS will be explained in such way.</callout>

### Changes in the quantity and/or quality of factors of production


#### Increase in the quantity and/or quality of factors of production â†’ rightward shift in LRAS / AS


1. **Factors of production** refer to the resources used in the production process: land, labor, capital, and entrepreneurship.
2. If the **quantity** of factors of production **increases** (larger labor force, discovery of new resources, or increased capital stock...), the economy's **production possibilities** **increases**.
3. Similarly, **improvements** in the **quality** of factors of production (higher-skilled workers, better technology, or more efficient capital...) also **increases** the economy's **productivity**.
4. Since **long-run aggregate supply** represents the economy's **potential output** at **full employment**, an **increase** in the **quantity** and/or **quality** of resources **increases** the economy's **potential** **output **through a growth in **production possibilities**.
5. This shifts the **LRAS curve** / **AS curve** to the **right** (LRAS1â†’LRAS2 / AS1â†’AS2).

![Figure 3: rightward shift in LRAS curve / Keynesian AS curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/fdcf8457da621e5d5ab113dbb4a17c15db51021b-2400x1048.png)

#### Decrease in the quantity and/or quality of factors of production â†’ leftward shift in LRAS / AS


1. **Factors of production** refer to the resources used in the production process: land, labor, capital, and entrepreneurship.
2. If the **quantity** of factors of production **decreases** (shrinking labor force due to aging population or emigration, depletion of natural resources, or reduced capital stock...), the economy's **production possibilities** **decreases**.
3. Similarly, **declines** in the **quality** of factors of production (poorly educated workforce, outdated technology, or inefficient capital...) also **decreases** the economy's **productivity**.
4. Since **long-run aggregate supply** represents the economy's **potential output** at **full employment**, a **decrease** in the **quantity** and/or **quality** of resources **decreases** the economy's **potential** **output **through a **decrease** in **production possibilities**.
5. This shifts the **LRAS curve** / **AS curve** to the **left** (LRAS1â†’LRAS2 / AS1â†’AS2).

![Figure 4: leftward shift in LRAS curve / Keynesian AS curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/ddc8f77fe4911b7680bdbb5099e718de860801b0-2400x1048.png)

### Improvements in technology


#### Improvements in technology â†’ rightward shift in LRAS / AS


1. **Technology** refers to the methods and innovations used in the production of goods and services.
2. **Advancements** in **technology** **improve** the **efficiency** of production processes, allowing **more** **output** to be produced with the **same** **quantity** of **resources**. 
3. Since **long-run aggregate supply** represents the **economyâ€™s** **potential** **output** at **full employment**, **technological** **improvements** **increase** the **potential output** by enhancing **productivity** and so expanding the **production possibilities**.
4. This leads to a **rightward shift** in the **LRAS curve **/ **AS curve** (LRAS1â†’LRAS2 / AS1â†’AS2).

![Figure 5: rightward shift in LRAS curve / Keynesian AS curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/fdcf8457da621e5d5ab113dbb4a17c15db51021b-2400x1048.png)

#### Declines in technology â†’ leftward shift in LRAS / AS


1. **Technology** refers to the methods and innovations used in the production of goods and services.
2. **Declines** in **technology** **reduce** the **efficiency** of production processes, causing **less** **output** to be produced with the **same** **quantity** of **resources**. 
3. Since **long-run aggregate supply** represents the **economyâ€™s** **potential** **output** at **full employment**, **technological** **declines** **decrease** the **potential output** by reducing **productivity** and so **decreasing** the **production possibilities**.
4. This leads to a **leftward shift** in the **LRAS curve **/ **AS curve** (LRAS1â†’LRAS2 / AS1â†’AS2).

![Figure 6: leftward shift in LRAS curve / Keynesian AS curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/ddc8f77fe4911b7680bdbb5099e718de860801b0-2400x1048.png)

### Increases in efficiency


#### Increases in efficiency â†’ rightward shift in LRAS / AS


1. **Efficiency** refers to the ability to maximise output from a given set of resources with minimal waste.
2. Improvements in **efficiency **(better resource allocation, enhanced worker training, optimised supply chain management...) allow the economy to produce **more** **output** **without** requiring **additional** **inputs**. 
3. Since **long-run aggregate supply** represents the **economyâ€™s** **potential** **output** at **full employment**, **improved efficiency** **increases** the **potential output** by enhancing **productivity** and so increasing the **production possibilities**.
4. This leads to a **rightward shift** in the **LRAS curve **/ **AS curve** (LRAS1â†’LRAS2 / AS1â†’AS2).

![Figure 7: rightward shift in LRAS curve / Keynesian AS curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/fdcf8457da621e5d5ab113dbb4a17c15db51021b-2400x1048.png)

#### Decreases in efficiency â†’ leftward shift in LRAS / AS


1. **Efficiency** refers to the ability to maximise output from a given set of resources with minimal waste.
2. Declines in **efficiency **(worse resource allocation, poorer worker training, sub-optimised supply chain management...) cause the economy to produce **less** **output** while using the same amount of **inputs**. 
3. Since **long-run aggregate supply** represents the **economyâ€™s** **potential** **output** at **full employment**, **reduced efficiency** **decreases** the **potential output** by **worsening** **productivity** and so **decreasing** the **production possibilities**.
4. This leads to a **leftward shift** in the **LRAS curve **/ **AS curve** (LRAS1â†’LRAS2 / AS1â†’AS2).

![Figure 8: leftward shift in LRAS curve / Keynesian AS curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/ddc8f77fe4911b7680bdbb5099e718de860801b0-2400x1048.png)

### Changes in institutions


<callout type="note">In later sections, we will discover what measures governments can take to affect the **long-run aggregate supply**.</callout>

1. When certain **political or economic priorities** shift, the **production possibilities** of an economy can change directly.
2. This can cause an **increase **in the economy's **potential output**, or a **decrease**.
3. Therefore, **changes in institutions can shift **the **long-run aggregate supply curves** in both directions.

<callout type="self_review">Go over all the **determinants of long-run aggregate supply** and try to explain their effect on the **aggregate supply over the long term **and the **LRAS **/ **AS curve** step-by-step.

If you master this, Paper 1 will become much easier!</callout>

<callout type="note">It is very uncommon for the **long-run aggregate supply** to decrease. Most of the times, it only increases.</callout>
